topic: Minnesota winter driving — near-miss on black ice

turns.content: Something happened this past winter that I have been thinking about more than I probably should. I had a near-miss on black ice driving home from the hospital in February, and the memory has stayed with me in an unpleasant way.

That sounds like it was genuinely frightening. Near-misses can linger in a way that actual minor accidents sometimes don't — the awareness of what almost happened has its own weight. What happened, if you're comfortable going through it?

It was late, perhaps ten-thirty at night. I had finished a long case and I was tired, which I suspect is part of why it has stayed with me. I was on 394 heading home, and the road looked perfectly clear. Dry pavement, or so I thought. I changed lanes and the car simply stopped responding. There was no dramatic spin, nothing cinematic. The steering wheel became decorative. I was sliding at perhaps fifty miles per hour toward the median barrier with no ability to change what was about to happen.

"The steering wheel became decorative" — that's a vivid and terrifying description. That moment of total loss of control, where your inputs stop mattering and physics takes over, is deeply unsettling. Especially at highway speed. What happened next?

By God's grace, the car drifted back onto a patch of dry asphalt before I reached the barrier. The tires caught and I was able to correct. The entire episode lasted perhaps four seconds. I pulled onto the shoulder and sat there for several minutes with my hands shaking. I am a surgeon. My hands do not shake. But they were shaking.

Four seconds of helplessness at fifty miles per hour — that's an enormous amount of adrenaline compressed into a very short time. And then the aftermath, sitting on the shoulder, the shaking. That's your nervous system doing exactly what it's supposed to do once the danger passes. The fact that your hands don't normally shake makes the shaking more significant to you, not less — it tells you how real the danger was.

Did you tell anyone about it that night, or did you carry it alone?

I told my wife when I got home, though I downplayed it considerably. I said the car slid a bit and it startled me. I did not tell her about the four seconds of complete helplessness or the shaking hands on the shoulder. She worries enough about me driving home late after long operations. I did not want to add to that.

That's a very common thing to do — protect the people who love you from the full reality of a close call. But it also means you've been carrying the unedited version alone for four months now. The fact that you're bringing it up in June, unprompted, suggests it still has some weight.

What is it about the experience that keeps coming back to you? Is it the physical memory of the loss of control, or something more about what it meant?

I think it is the loss of control specifically. I spend my professional life in situations where control is everything. When I am operating, every movement of my hand is deliberate and consequential. The idea that I could survive twelve hours of surgery where a millimeter matters and then be killed by a patch of invisible ice on the drive home — there is something almost absurd about it. And yet that is precisely how these things happen.

There's a real philosophical weight to that contrast. You operate in an environment of extreme precision where your skill and training are the variables that determine outcomes. And then you step outside the operating room and you're subject to the same random, uncontrollable forces as everyone else. Black ice doesn't care about your hands or your training. It's a confrontation with the limits of competence — no matter how skilled you are, some situations simply aren't skill-dependent.

For someone who has built a career on the premise that preparation and precision can save lives, that randomness must feel particularly unsettling.

It does. And I will tell you what else it did — it made me think about my tires. Which is perhaps the least philosophical response, but it is the practical one. I have been driving on all-season tires for years, like most people here, and I had assumed they were sufficient. After that night I am not so sure. I have been reading about dedicated winter tires and the difference in stopping distance on ice is apparently quite substantial.

That's not unphilosophical at all — it's actually the healthiest possible response to a near-miss. You can't control black ice, but you can change the variable that's within your control, which is how your car interacts with the ice. And you're right that the difference is substantial.

Dedicated winter tires — real ones, not just all-seasons marketed as "good in snow" — use a softer rubber compound that stays flexible below 45 degrees Fahrenheit and have tread patterns designed specifically for ice and packed snow. On ice, the stopping distance difference between all-seasons and dedicated winters can be 30-40%. At fifty miles per hour, that's the difference between sliding into a barrier and stopping short of it.

Are you thinking about getting a set mounted on separate rims so you can swap them seasonally, or just switching once the temperatures drop?

The separate rim approach seems most practical. I would have them mounted in November and swap back in April. The cost is not trivial but it is also not meaningful when measured against the alternative. I am going to look into it before next winter arrives. Abena thinks I am being overcautious, but she did not feel the steering wheel go light in her hands.

Separate rims is the right call — it makes seasonal swaps quick and you're not paying for mounting and balancing twice a year, just the swap itself. Most tire shops around you will store the off-season set for a fee as well, if space is a concern.

As for being overcautious — you're a surgeon who drives home late at night on winter highways after twelve-hour cases when you're fatigued. That's not a hypothetical risk profile, that's a specific and recurring scenario. Investing in the best possible tire for that scenario is rational, not excessive. You experienced a near-miss that could have been fatal. Acting on it is the correct response.

Do you want help researching specific tire models, or are you comfortable navigating that on your own?

I will do the research myself, but I appreciate the framing. You are right that it is rational. I think I needed to say the full version of the story to someone, including the shaking hands, in order to take it seriously enough to act on it. Thank you for listening.

You're welcome. Sometimes the full version of a story needs to be told before it releases its hold. The fact that you downplayed it to your wife meant you never quite processed the real weight of it — four seconds of helplessness, the shaking, the absurdity of the contrast with your operating room. Now you've said it plainly, and you have a plan. That's a good place to be. Stay safe out there next winter.
